_meta.interaction: Maturity self-check (kind: maturity-check) — NON-gating, neutral framing
_meta.source: AI adoption maturity model „Holak Scale”: v2.1e (organization, 0–11) + v2.1p (individual, 0–11). Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka and quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka-private.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL self-check statements are SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). An indicative diagnosis — it cannot be „failed”. Scoring heuristic is simplified (count of checks → level 0–11) and subject to subject-matter-expert (SME) review.

intro: The Holak Scale is an AI adoption maturity model: it helps you name where you are today and what the realistic next step is. This module is a self-check — not a test. There is no „pass/fail” result; there is an indicative level and a hint on how to move up.

blocks.text: The Holak Scale describes AI-usage maturity across 12 levels (0–11) — from one-off experiments to a conscious, measured, continuously improved practice. It helps avoid two traps: „we use AI, so we're mature” and „it's just a toy”.

items: Organizational version (v2.1e) — AI adoption maturity in a team or company.
Individual version (v2.1p) — the maturity of your own way of working with AI.
We assess both dimensions separately: an organization may be higher or lower than a single person.

blocks.title: Where the name and the model come from
blocks.text: The Holak Scale was devised by Grzegorz Holak with support from Konrad Gomulski — hence the name. Both are Senior Test Development Engineers at Sii Poland and members of SJSI, and co-founders of quality-blog.eu; Grzegorz Holak is additionally a Test Architect and AI Ambassador at SCIB. Sources: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka (organization) and quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka-private (individual). We use it as a self-reflection tool, not a certification.

screens.title: How to tell your level: assessment axes and thresholds

blocks.text: You recognize higher levels not by the number of tools, but by HOW you use AI. Key axes (from roughly level 4 up): context management, awareness of model limits, token economy, output verifiability, decision reversibility, and data security and privacy.

items: Barrier — what blocks entry to a given level.
Success — what truly works once the level is reached.
Trap — a false sense of maturity that sets development back.

blocks.title: Two critical thresholds
blocks.text: Threshold 8→9: moving from „individual good practice” to wiring AI into a process with a clear assistant/decision-maker split and an audit trail. Threshold 10→11: from one-off improvements to continuous improvement and calibration of your own AI use.

Next you'll check the statements that are true today — separately for the organization and for yourself. The number of checks gives an indicative level 0–11 on each scale plus a „where you are / how to move up” hint. Check honestly only what is actually in place.

blocks.title: It's a diagnosis, not a grade
blocks.text: There are no correct or incorrect answers here and no pass threshold. The result does not affect path completion or the final test — it's a mirror, not an exam.

interaction.title: Holak Scale — AI maturity self-check
interaction.intro: Check the statements true today. Separately for the organization (v2.1e) and for yourself (v2.1p). After checking you'll see an indicative level 0–11 and a next step — with no pass/fail grading.

scales.label: Organization (Holak Scale v2.1e)
scales.intro: How mature is AI adoption in your team/company? Check what is actually in place.

statements.text: Some people use AI for individual tasks.
There is informal sharing of good AI-usage practices.
An acceptable-use policy for AI has been written (data, privacy, IP).
Selected processes have defined, permitted AI uses.
AI outputs in critical processes are mandatorily verified by a human.
Teams measure AI's impact on quality and time, not just the fact of usage.
There is conscious management of context and token cost in repeatable tasks.
AI-supported decisions are reversible and leave an audit trail.
AI is wired into processes with a clear split: assistant vs decision-maker.
The organization regularly calibrates and audits its own AI use.
AI adoption is part of strategy and continuous improvement, not a one-off initiative.

bands.label: Level 0–2: Grassroots experiments
bands.advice: Barrier: no shared rules or goal. Next step: write a simple AI acceptable-use policy (data, privacy) and pick 1–2 pilot processes.

bands.label: Level 3–5: First frameworks
bands.advice: Success: there are rules and permitted uses. Next step: introduce mandatory verification of outputs in critical processes and start measuring impact.

bands.label: Level 6–8: Conscious processes
bands.advice: Success: you measure impact and manage context. Threshold 8→9: wire AI into a process with a clear assistant/decision-maker split and an audit trail for decisions.

bands.label: Level 9–11: Mature adoption
bands.advice: Trap: treating maturity as a finished state rather than continuous improvement. Threshold 10→11: make calibration and auditing of AI use part of strategy and team routine.

scales.label: Individual (Holak Scale v2.1p)
scales.intro: How mature is your own way of working with AI? Check what you do day to day.

statements.text: I use AI for specific, repeatable tasks.
I deliberately frame the prompt: role, goal, context, output format.
I recognize that output can be wrong despite a credible form.
I verify critical answers against a source before I use them.
I match the tool/model to the task; I don't use one for everything.
I manage context: I provide only what's needed and watch the cost.
I know when NOT to use AI: missing data, high risk, irreversibility.
I design work so AI-supported decisions are reversible and checkable.
I share practices and calibrate output assessment together with others.
I measure AI's real impact on my quality and time, and adjust my approach.
I treat working with AI as continuous improvement of my craft.

bands.label: Level 0–2: First uses
bands.advice: Barrier: treating AI as an oracle. Next step: start framing prompts deliberately and verifying every answer you intend to use.

bands.label: Level 3–5: Conscious user
bands.advice: Success: you verify output and match the tool to the task. Next step: learn to manage context and cost, and to recognize when NOT to use AI.

bands.label: Level 6–8: Practitioner
bands.advice: Success: you know the limits and manage context. Threshold 8→9: design work so decisions are reversible and checkable, and share practices with your team.

bands.label: Level 9–11: Craft mastery
bands.advice: Trap: routine without measurement or reflection. Threshold 10→11: measure AI's real impact on your work and treat your craft as continuous improvement.

points: The Holak Scale measures HOW you use AI (context, verifiability, reversibility), not the number of tools.
You assess the organization (v2.1e) and the individual (v2.1p) separately — they may be at different levels.
It's an indicative diagnosis: no pass/fail, no effect on completion — it's there to point out the next step.
checklist: Have you named your indicative level for the organization and for yourself?
Do you know the nearest barrier to overcome?
Do you know one concrete next step to move up?
summary.nextStep: Next step: pick one barrier from a higher level and plan a single small, reversible experiment.
